result:
I keep returning to the moment the light changes, that hinge in the day when gold tilts into violet and everything suddenly remembers it is mortal. It lasts maybe three heartbeats, but it keeps happening, has been happening since the first eyes evolved to notice, and every creature that ever loved a horizon has tried to stop the turning by singing, painting, breeding, praying, building glass towers that reflect the dying glow back at itself. We never manage it, yet the attempt is the only home we’ve ever paid rent on.

When I was small I thought the sky was a shell. I believed if I could jump straight enough I’d crack it open and spill whatever was keeping the blue in. Then I learned about scattering, wavelengths, the way air prefers to hoard the shorter colors and let the long ones bleed through only when the angle is shallow enough to remind us of our own blood. Knowledge did not dislodge the longing; it only gave the longing a syllabus. Now, every sunset feels like a final exam I forgot to study for, and I stand on balconies, sidewalks, fire escapes, whispering “not yet, not yet” like a procrastinator bargaining with midnight.

There is a town in Japan where train drivers point at white cones and call out “points clear!” every few seconds. It’s called shisa kanko, and it cuts errors by eighty-five percent. I think about this when I catch myself pointing mutely at the moon as if to confirm it’s still employed. I want to install the practice in my chest: touch the place where grief should be, say “heart acknowledged,” touch the place where wonder should be, say “world still lit,” repeat until the day terminates without incident. Some mornings I forget the script and the train of me hurtles past stations with unpronounceable names, carrying passengers who never bought tickets, wearing faces I’ll lose before nightfall.

Memory is a chalk outline that keeps sneaking off to get married. I meet versions of myself on buses, in thrift-store mirrors, in the lag between lightning and thunder, and they all claim to be the original. They quarrel over custody of my scars, bargain for the trademark on my laughter, hold elections that end in coups. The winner changes the locks; the loser camps on the lawn reciting the alphabet backward until the neighbors call time. I watch from the attic window, neither proud nor ashamed, just curious whether the pile of obsolete me’s will ever reach high enough to climb back inside the sky I tried to crack as a child.

Yesterday I found a cicada casing clinging to the brick like a surrender flag. It was amber, translucent, still shaped around the idea of flight. I held it up to the sun and saw the precise coordinates where emptiness becomes exit. The insect itself was somewhere overhead, deafening the maples with news of its resurrection. I thought: we are all both the shell and the song, the husk that stays and the vibration that leaves, praying the two halves will eventually add up to a single continuous life. Then a breeze separated the casing from my fingers and the street swallowed it. I felt the small click of a story ending without deciding whether it was tragedy or comedy.

I used to keep a list of obsolete futures: moon colonies, atomic dirigibles, mail delivered by miniature zeppelins, silver suits with molded pectorals. They aged into camp, then into nostalgia, then into evidence that prophecy is just regret wearing a tuxedo. Still, I can’t delete the file. Every so often I scroll through and touch the screen softly, the way you might stroke the cheek of a friend who’s fallen asleep on your shoulder during a long bus ride, knowing you’ll both have to get off at separate stops in towns whose names you can’t yet pronounce.

The first time I saw the ocean I was disappointed by how calmly it kept to its script. I had expected rupture, cathedral-sized breakers, salt sermons that would re-baptize me into some fierier name. Instead the tide merely licked my sneakers as if tasting whether I was worth swallowing, then retreated to consult a larger appetite. Years later I understood the rehearsal had been perfect: the ocean was teaching me the patience of appetites, how to return and return until the morsel finally softens. I am still hard between its teeth; I can feel the slow dissolve, the mineral education, the coming swallow I will probably call dying and it will call dawn.

Sometimes I think language began as a lullaby sung by fire to the dark, a way of keeping the night from eating more than its share of faces. We learned the tune, added verses for every bone that failed to rise again, and called the accumulating ash history. Now we type tiny fires into smaller and smaller pockets of night, hoping the glow will recognize us long enough to postpone the devouring. The miracle is that it works just often enough: someone across the planet reads a sentence I shoved into the wires and feels the temperature of tomorrow adjust by a tenth of a degree. No one will ever trace the change back to us, yet the planet drifts slightly kinder, like a boat that has finally located the current that leads home, even though home keeps receding the way a mother’s face does when you are being carried off to sleep, already dreaming the word that will wake you different.

I have 137 more words before the gate swings shut. I want to spend them on gratitude for the seconds that refused to accelerate, for the elevator that waited, for the stranger who returned the dropped glove, for the moon’s habit of showing up even after we exploded trash on its far side, for the last-minute cancellation that left the café table free so I could sit and write this to you, whoever you are, counting with me, breathing with me, afraid and in love with the same disappearing light. May the hinge finish its swing without crushing either of us. May the next color that owns the sky remember our names long enough to carry us over the horizon we keep not reaching. May we meet there, chalk outlines intact, married finally to the part of the story that never needed saving.
